clinicalStem: A 68-year-old man is seen in the cardiology outpatient clinic after being newly diagnosed with heart failure (HF) following an episode of acute pulmonary oedema. Echocardiography confirms an LVEF of 32%. He asks his doctor about his outlook. Which of the following statements most accurately reflects the prognosis of heart failure in general?
options: The 1-year mortality after diagnosis is approximately 20% and the 5-year mortality is approximately 53%
The 1-year mortality is less than 5% with modern therapy, making the prognosis similar to that of hypertension
HFpEF carries a substantially better 5-year survival compared with HFrEF in most large observational studies
Improvement in prognosis over recent decades has been seen equally in HFrEF and HFpEF patients
Women with HF have a worse survival than men because they receive less evidence-based therapy
explanation: According to the lecture, the 1-year and 5-year mortality rates after diagnosis for all types of HF are 20% and 53% respectively, indicating HF carries a poor prognosis overall. The improvement in prognosis has been confined to HFrEF, not HFpEF. Despite receiving less evidence-based treatment, women actually have better survival than men.
wrongAnswerExplanations: This is correct: the 1-year mortality is 20% and 5-year mortality is 53% for all HF types.
Incorrect. HF carries a high mortality — 20% at 1 year and 53% at 5 years; it is far worse than hypertension alone.
Incorrect. HFpEF is generally considered to confer a better survival than HFrEF, but most observational studies show this difference is negligible.
Incorrect. The improvement in prognosis has been confined to those with HFrEF; HFpEF prognosis has not improved to the same extent.
Incorrect. Women with HF have a better survival than men despite receiving less evidence-based treatment.
clinicalPearl: After a new diagnosis of heart failure, the 1-year mortality is approximately 20% and the 5-year mortality is approximately 53%, underscoring the severity of this condition.
keyClue: Newly diagnosed HF — the question asks about prognosis statistics from the lecture material.
commonExamTrap: Students often assume HFpEF carries a clearly better prognosis than HFrEF — observational studies show the difference is negligible.
missedConcept: Overall HF mortality data: 20% at 1 year and 53% at 5 years for all types of HF.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old woman with ischaemic cardiomyopathy has NYHA Class IV symptoms despite maximum tolerated doses of ACE inhibitor, beta-blocker, mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, and dapagliflozin. Her LVEF is 22% on echocardiography. She has required three hospitalisations in the past 10 months for intravenous diuretics. Her 6-minute walk test distance is 210 metres. She is now listed for cardiac transplantation but no donor organ is available. A left ventricular assist device (LVAD) is implanted to keep her alive until a donor organ becomes available. This use of mechanical circulatory support is best described as:
options: Bridge to transplantation (BTT)
Bridge to decision (BTD)
Destination therapy (DT)
Bridge to candidacy (BTC)
Bridge to recovery (BTR)
explanation: Bridge to transplantation (BTT) refers to the use of MCS (LVAD, BiVAD or TAH) to keep a patient alive who is otherwise at high risk of death before transplantation until a donor organ becomes available. This patient meets criteria for advanced HF (NYHA III-IV, LVEF <30%, multiple hospitalisations, 6MWT <300 m) and is already listed — the LVAD is used to bridge her until a donor is found.
wrongAnswerExplanations: This is correct: BTT is MCS used to keep an already-listed patient alive until a donor organ becomes available.

Incorrect. BTD/BTB refers to short-term MCS (ECMO or Impella) in cardiogenic shock until haemodynamics are stabilised and long-term options can be evaluated — she is haemodynamically stable and already listed.
Incorrect. Destination therapy refers to long-term LVAD use as an alternative to transplantation in patients who are not eligible for transplant — this patient is already listed.
Incorrect. Bridge to candidacy refers to using MCS to improve organ function to make an ineligible patient eligible for transplant — she is already listed and eligible.
Incorrect. Bridge to recovery is MCS used until cardiac function recovers sufficiently to remove the device — ischaemic cardiomyopathy at this severity is unlikely to recover.
clinicalPearl: Bridge to transplantation (BTT) uses LVAD/BiVAD to keep an already-listed patient alive until a donor organ is available, whereas destination therapy uses LVAD permanently for those ineligible for transplant.
keyClue: Patient is already listed for transplant — the LVAD is keeping her alive while awaiting a donor.
commonExamTrap: Confusing BTT (patient already listed, awaiting organ) with BTC (patient not yet eligible, device used to improve organ function to gain candidacy).
missedConcept: Terminology for mechanical circulatory support indications: BTD, BTC, BTT, BTR, and destination therapy each have specific definitions.

clinicalStem: A 61-year-old man has ischaemic cardiomyopathy with LVEF 28% on optimal medical therapy including sacubitril/valsartan, carvedilol, eplerenone and dapagliflozin for the past 6 months. A 12-lead ECG shows sinus rhythm with a QRS duration of 158 ms (left bundle branch block morphology). He has NYHA Class II symptoms. Which of the following device therapies is most appropriate at this stage?
options: Cardiac resynchronisation therapy with defibrillator (CRT-D) — Class I recommendation
Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) alone — Class I recommendation
CRT-D — Class IIa recommendation
ICD alone — Class IIa recommendation
No device therapy is indicated until LVEF improves below 25%
explanation: In HFrEF with LVEF ≤35%, sinus rhythm, and QRS ≥150 ms with LBBB morphology, CRT-D carries a Class I (ESC) recommendation — this is the highest evidence level for device therapy in this patient group. An ICD alone would not address the dyssynchrony. The criteria are all met: sinus rhythm, LVEF ≤35%, QRS ≥150 ms.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Sinus rhythm + LVEF ≤35% + QRS ≥150 ms qualifies for Class I CRT-D.
Incorrect. ICD alone is Class I for ischaemic aetiology (LVEF ≤35%, QRS <130 ms), but when QRS ≥150 ms is present in sinus rhythm, CRT-D supersedes ICD alone.
Incorrect. CRT-D is Class IIa for QRS 130–149 ms; for QRS ≥150 ms in sinus rhythm with LBBB it is Class I.
Incorrect. ICD alone as Class IIa applies to non-ischaemic cardiomyopathy — this patient has ischaemic disease and QRS ≥150 ms.
Incorrect. There is no requirement for LVEF to drop below 25% before device therapy; the threshold for ICD/CRT is LVEF ≤35%.
clinicalPearl: In HFrEF with LVEF ≤35%, sinus rhythm, and QRS ≥150 ms (LBBB morphology), CRT-D is a Class I ESC recommendation; for QRS 130–149 ms it is Class IIa.
keyClue: Sinus rhythm, LVEF 28%, QRS 158 ms with LBBB — all criteria for Class I CRT-D are met.
commonExamTrap: Selecting ICD alone without considering CRT when QRS ≥150 ms is present; or applying Class IIa criteria when Class I criteria are clearly met.
missedConcept: The QRS duration threshold distinguishes Class I (≥150 ms) from Class IIa (130–149 ms) CRT indications in HFrEF with sinus rhythm.

clinicalStem: A 72-year-old man is admitted to the emergency department with worsening breathlessness over 6 hours. He has a history of ischaemic cardiomyopathy. On examination: blood pressure 76/50 mmHg, heart rate 118 bpm, cool and mottled peripheries, oliguria, SpO2 88% on room air. The JVP is elevated and there are bibasal crackles. An urgent echocardiogram shows LVEF 18% with no new wall motion abnormality. Which of the following best describes the haemodynamic profile and the primary treatment approach?
options: Wet and cold — cardiogenic shock; inotropic agents/vasopressors and consideration of short-term mechanical circulatory support
Wet and warm — acute pulmonary oedema; IV diuretics and vasodilators
Dry and cold — isolated RV failure; IV fluids and inotropes
Wet and cold — acute decompensated HF; IV diuretics and loop diuretics alone
Wet and warm — hypertensive emergency; IV nitrates and sodium nitroprusside
explanation: This patient is haemodynamically unstable with systemic hypoperfusion (hypotension, cool peripheries, oliguria) and signs of congestion (raised JVP, crackles) — the 'wet and cold' profile of cardiogenic shock. The main treatment includes inotropic agents and/or vasopressors, with short-term mechanical circulatory support if needed. The in-hospital mortality of cardiogenic shock is high and urgent action is required.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. This is wet and cold cardiogenic shock: congestion plus hypoperfusion, requiring inotropes/vasopressors and possible MCS.
Incorrect. Wet and warm describes acute pulmonary oedema with preserved/high SBP — this patient is hypotensive with cool peripheries.
Incorrect. Dry and cold with isolated RV failure has increased CVP without left-sided congestion and low SBP; this patient has bibasal crackles indicating left-sided congestion.
Incorrect. Diuretics alone are insufficient for cardiogenic shock with hypotension — inotropes/vasopressors are required.
Incorrect. There is no hypertension here — BP is 76/50 mmHg; vasodilators would worsen shock.
clinicalPearl: Cardiogenic shock presents as 'wet and cold': congestion plus systemic hypoperfusion. Treat with inotropes/vasopressors and consider short-term mechanical circulatory support urgently.
keyClue: BP 76/50 mmHg + cool peripheries + elevated JVP + bibasal crackles = wet and cold cardiogenic shock.
commonExamTrap: Treating as simple acute pulmonary oedema with diuretics when hypotension and hypoperfusion indicate cardiogenic shock requiring inotropic support.
missedConcept: The distinction between wet-and-warm (acute pulmonary oedema) and wet-and-cold (cardiogenic shock) determines whether vasodilators or inotropes are the priority.
redFlagReason: Cardiogenic shock with BP 76/50 mmHg and systemic hypoperfusion is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate inotropic/vasopressor support and consideration of mechanical circulatory support.

clinicalStem: A 66-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of progressive, painless jaundice, 8 kg weight loss, and pale stools. He denies alcohol use and takes no regular medications. On examination: scleral icterus, a non-tender palpable mass in the right upper quadrant, and no hepatomegaly. Urinalysis shows bilirubin present but urobilinogen is absent. LFTs show markedly elevated ALP and GGT with conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia up to 320 μmol/L. PT is elevated and corrects with vitamin K. Which diagnosis is most consistent with the prolonged absence of urobilinogen in the urine?
options: Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas causing complete biliary obstruction
Cholangitis due to common bile duct stones
Alcoholic hepatitis causing hepatocellular jaundice
Haemolytic anaemia causing pre-hepatic jaundice
Gilbert's syndrome with intercurrent illness
explanation: Complete obstruction of the biliary tree leads to absence of urobilinogen from urine. Prolonged absence of urobilinogen specifically indicates pancreatic head malignancy rather than stones, because stones allow some bile through intermittently. The clinical picture — painless jaundice, weight loss, palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier's sign), pale stools, conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia, and PT correctable with vitamin K — all point to extrahepatic (post-hepatic) obstruction from pancreatic carcinoma.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Prolonged complete absence of urobilinogen indicates a persistent complete obstruction, most commonly pancreatic head malignancy rather than stones.
Incorrect. CBD stones cause intermittent obstruction allowing some bile through, so urobilinogen would not be persistently absent; typically associated with colicky pain.
Incorrect. Hepatocellular jaundice causes increased urobilinogen in urine (impaired re-excretion), not absence; PT would not fully correct with vitamin K.
Incorrect. Haemolytic jaundice (pre-hepatic) causes increased urobilinogen (not absent), dark stools, and unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia.
Incorrect. Gilbert's syndrome causes mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia with normal urobilinogen; it does not cause pale stools, elevated ALP, or PT prolongation.
clinicalPearl: Prolonged absence of urinary urobilinogen in a jaundiced patient suggests complete biliary obstruction, and strongly favours pancreatic head malignancy over stones (which allow intermittent bile passage).
keyClue: Prolonged absence of urobilinogen in urine in the context of complete, painless obstructive jaundice.
commonExamTrap: Choosing CBD stones without recognising that stones cause intermittent, not prolonged, absence of urobilinogen.
missedConcept: Only conjugated bilirubin appears in urine; complete biliary obstruction abolishes urobilinogen, and its prolonged absence points to malignancy over gallstones.

clinicalStem: A 24-year-old woman presents with mild jaundice noticed by her family. She reports dark urine but no pale stools. She has a family history of haemolytic anaemia. Examination reveals mild scleral icterus and splenomegaly. Investigations: serum bilirubin 85 μmol/L (predominantly unconjugated), ALP normal, PT normal, reticulocyte count elevated, positive direct Coombs test, no bilirubin detected in urine. Which type of jaundice does this presentation represent, and which investigation finding best confirms this?
options: Pre-hepatic haemolytic jaundice — absence of urine bilirubin (acholuric jaundice) with elevated urobilinogen
Hepatocellular jaundice — elevated ALP and mixed bilirubin with elevated AST/ALT
Post-hepatic cholestatic jaundice — conjugated bilirubin in urine with pale stools
Intrahepatic cholestasis — elevated GGT and direct bilirubin, PT correctable with vitamin K
Pre-hepatic jaundice — presence of bilirubin in urine confirming unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia
explanation: Haemolytic (pre-hepatic) jaundice is characterised by unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia, which is water-insoluble and bound to albumin; therefore it cannot be filtered into urine. This is termed 'acholuric jaundice' — jaundice without bilirubin in the urine. Increased red cell breakdown produces excess urobilinogen that is excreted in the urine, and stools are dark (excess stercobilinogen). Normal ALP and PT further confirm the pre-hepatic location.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Pre-hepatic jaundice is acholuric (no urine bilirubin) with elevated urobilinogen in urine; unconjugated bilirubin cannot enter urine.
Incorrect. Hepatocellular jaundice causes elevated ALP, GGT, AST, and ALT with mixed bilirubin — none of these are elevated here.
Incorrect. Post-hepatic cholestatic jaundice causes conjugated bilirubin in urine and pale stools — this patient has dark (not pale) stools and no urine bilirubin.
Incorrect. Intrahepatic cholestasis elevates GGT and conjugated bilirubin; this patient has unconjugated predominance and normal liver enzymes.
Incorrect. In pre-hepatic jaundice, unconjugated bilirubin is water-insoluble and cannot appear in urine — the absence of urine bilirubin is the key finding, not its presence.
clinicalPearl: Haemolytic (pre-hepatic) jaundice is acholuric — no bilirubin in urine — because unconjugated bilirubin is water-insoluble; urobilinogen is elevated and stools are dark.
keyClue: No bilirubin in urine (acholuric jaundice) with unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia and elevated reticulocytes.
commonExamTrap: Assuming bilirubin must always appear in the urine in jaundice — only conjugated (water-soluble) bilirubin can be excreted renally.
missedConcept: Only conjugated bilirubin is water-soluble and appears in urine. Unconjugated bilirubin in haemolytic jaundice produces acholuric jaundice.

clinicalStem: A 74-year-old man with known aortic stenosis (AS) has been followed up with serial echocardiography for 3 years, remaining asymptomatic. At his latest clinic visit he reports new onset of exertional dyspnoea and one episode of exertional syncope last week. Echocardiography shows a calcified aortic valve with a peak gradient of 72 mmHg and valve area of 0.7 cm². His LVEF is 55%. Which of the following statements about his prognosis is most accurate?
options: Once symptoms develop in severe AS, the prognosis is poor and untreated patients typically die within 3–5 years
Symptomatic severe AS carries the same prognosis as asymptomatic severe AS and close monitoring is sufficient
The development of syncope alone in AS does not affect prognosis and surgery can be deferred indefinitely
Since LVEF is preserved at 55%, surgical or transcatheter aortic valve replacement is not yet indicated
Medical therapy with ACE inhibitors can significantly slow progression and improve survival in symptomatic AS
explanation: Aortic stenosis typically remains asymptomatic for many years but deteriorates rapidly when symptoms develop. Once symptoms of angina, syncope, or heart failure appear, untreated patients usually die within 3–5 years of presentation. This patient has developed two of the three cardinal symptoms (syncope and dyspnoea), making prompt surgical or transcatheter aortic valve replacement (AVR/TAVI) an urgent indication.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Symptomatic severe AS carries a poor prognosis with death within 3–5 years if untreated — prompt AVR is indicated.
Incorrect. Asymptomatic severe AS has a good immediate prognosis with conservative management; symptomatic severe AS requires prompt AVR.
Incorrect. Syncope is one of the three cardinal symptoms of severe AS indicating prompt surgery — it should not be ignored.
Incorrect. Preserved LVEF does not preclude AVR in symptomatic severe AS; symptoms are the primary indication regardless of EF.
Incorrect. No medical treatments have been shown to prevent progression of AS or improve survival; the definitive treatment is AVR or TAVI.
clinicalPearl: Symptomatic severe AS (any of angina, syncope, or heart failure) carries a poor prognosis — death within 3–5 years untreated — and is an indication for prompt AVR or TAVI regardless of preserved EF.
keyClue: New onset exertional syncope and dyspnoea in a patient with known severe AS.
commonExamTrap: Assuming that preserved LVEF means surgery can be deferred in symptomatic severe AS — symptoms, not EF alone, trigger prompt intervention.
missedConcept: AS remains asymptomatic for years but deteriorates rapidly once symptoms develop; the symptom triad (angina, syncope, dyspnoea) each indicate poor prognosis without intervention.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman with known rheumatic mitral stenosis (valve area 1.4 cm²) presents to the emergency department with sudden onset right-sided weakness and dysphasia over 2 hours. She has had palpitations for 3 days. Her ECG confirms new-onset atrial fibrillation. CT head shows no haemorrhage. Which complication of mitral stenosis is the most likely cause of her acute neurological deficit, and what is its mechanism?
options: Cardioembolic stroke due to left atrial thrombus formation from AF-related atrial stasis
Paradoxical embolism through a patent foramen ovale from deep vein thrombosis
Hypertensive intracerebral haemorrhage secondary to chronic pressure overload
Infective endocarditis with septic embolism to the cerebral circulation
Pulmonary embolism presenting as syncope with focal neurology from cerebral hypoperfusion
explanation: AF is very common in mitral stenosis, and its onset often precipitates thromboembolism. Atrial fibrillation causes atrial stasis, especially in the enlarged left atrium of MS, leading to thrombus formation (particularly in the left atrial appendage) and systemic embolism. The clinical presentation of sudden focal neurology without haemorrhage on CT confirms acute ischaemic stroke from cardioembolic thromboembolism. This is a well-recognised, dangerous complication of MS.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. AF-induced left atrial stasis in rheumatic MS is the most common mechanism for systemic thromboembolism and cardioembolic stroke.
Incorrect. Paradoxical embolism requires a right-to-left shunt and DVT — there is no evidence of either here.
Incorrect. Hypertensive haemorrhage would show hyperdense lesion on CT; this patient has a normal CT head and no mention of hypertension.
Incorrect. Infective endocarditis is possible but would typically present with fever, bacteraemia, and signs of systemic infection — not described here.
Incorrect. Pulmonary embolism does not cause focal ischaemic stroke; cerebral hypoperfusion from PE typically causes global, not focal, neurological deficits.
clinicalPearl: AF onset in mitral stenosis is a dangerous complication that frequently precipitates systemic thromboembolism due to left atrial stasis — anticoagulation is mandatory.
keyClue: New-onset AF in rheumatic mitral stenosis followed immediately by acute focal neurological deficit.
commonExamTrap: Forgetting that mitral stenosis is the classic valvular lesion that predisposes to stroke when AF develops, due to profound left atrial enlargement and stasis.
missedConcept: MS + AF = high thromboembolic risk; anticoagulation (warfarin — DOACs are not approved for this indication) is required.
redFlagReason: Acute ischaemic stroke is a neurological emergency requiring immediate assessment, imaging, and consideration of reperfusion therapy within the treatment window.

subtopic: Indications for surgery in asymptomatic AR
clinicalStem: A 45-year-old man with known chronic aortic regurgitation (AR) due to bicuspid aortic valve has been under annual echocardiographic surveillance. He remains completely asymptomatic. His most recent echocardiogram shows: LVEF 54%, LV end-diastolic dimension (EDD) 58 mm, moderate-to-severe AR on Doppler. He has no systemic hypertension. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?
options: Proceed to aortic valve replacement because LV EDD has reached 55 mm or more in an asymptomatic patient
Continue annual surveillance as LVEF is preserved and the patient is asymptomatic
Start nifedipine to reduce afterload and defer surgery until symptoms develop
Start an ACE inhibitor to slow LV dilatation and reduce the rate of AR progression
Refer for aortic valve replacement only when LVEF drops below 40%
explanation: In asymptomatic chronic AR, annual echocardiographic follow-up is recommended to detect evidence of progressive LV dilatation. According to the lecture, if the LV end-diastolic diameter (EDD) increases to 55 mm or more, AVR should be undertaken even in the absence of symptoms. This patient's EDD is 58 mm, which crosses this threshold, making AVR appropriate now to prevent irreversible LV dysfunction.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. EDD ≥55 mm in asymptomatic AR is an indication for AVR to prevent irreversible LV damage.
Incorrect. Although asymptomatic, the LV EDD of 58 mm meets the threshold for AVR — continued surveillance alone would risk irreversible LV dysfunction.
Incorrect. Nifedipine is recommended in AR only if systemic hypertension is present; this patient is normotensive and has already reached the surgical threshold.
Incorrect. ACE inhibitors reduce afterload in hypertensive AR patients but do not change surgical thresholds and do not prevent disease progression sufficiently to defer surgery.
Incorrect. Waiting for LVEF to drop below 40% before intervening risks irreversible cardiomyopathy; the EDD criterion (≥55 mm) allows earlier intervention.
clinicalPearl: In asymptomatic AR followed annually by echocardiography, an LV end-diastolic diameter ≥55 mm is an indication for AVR regardless of symptom status, to prevent irreversible LV dysfunction.
keyClue: LV EDD of 58 mm in an asymptomatic patient with AR — the 55 mm threshold for AVR has been exceeded.
commonExamTrap: Waiting for symptoms before recommending AVR in AR — the EDD ≥55 mm criterion allows intervention before symptoms and irreversible LV damage occur.
missedConcept: Asymptomatic AR with EDD ≥55 mm or progressive LV enlargement on follow-up echocardiography warrants AVR regardless of symptom status.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old woman was diagnosed with acute rheumatic fever at the age of 11, complicated by carditis with residual mitral valve disease (persistent valvar disease confirmed on echocardiography). She has been on benzathine benzylpenicillin prophylaxis since then. She now asks how long she needs to continue prophylaxis. Which of the following best describes the recommended duration of secondary prophylaxis?
options: At least 10 years since the last episode or until age 40 (whichever is longer), sometimes lifelong
5 years since the last episode or until age 21 (whichever is longer)
10 years since the last episode or until age 21 (whichever is longer)
Prophylaxis can be stopped now as she has had no further streptococcal infections for 8 years
Prophylaxis is only required until cardiac surgery corrects the valve lesion
explanation: According to the lecture, patients with rheumatic fever who have carditis with residual valvar disease (persistent valvar disease) require prophylaxis for 10 years since the last episode or until age 40 — whichever is longer — and sometimes lifelong prophylaxis is indicated. She has persistent mitral valve disease, placing her in the highest-risk category requiring the longest duration of prophylaxis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Rheumatic fever with carditis and residual valvar disease requires prophylaxis for 10 years/until age 40 (whichever is longer), sometimes lifelong.
Incorrect. The 5-year/until age 21 rule applies to rheumatic fever without carditis — not to those with residual valvar disease.
Incorrect. The 10-year/until age 21 rule applies to rheumatic fever with carditis but without residual valve disease — this patient has residual valvar disease.
Incorrect. Absence of further streptococcal infections does not justify stopping prophylaxis in residual valvar disease; the risk of recurrence remains.
Incorrect. Valvar surgery does not eliminate the risk of recurrent rheumatic fever from streptococcal pharyngitis; prophylaxis continues post-operatively.
clinicalPearl: Secondary rheumatic fever prophylaxis duration depends on severity: residual valvar disease requires 10 years/age 40 (whichever longer), sometimes lifelong; carditis without residual disease: 10 years/age 21; no carditis: 5 years/age 21.
keyClue: Rheumatic fever with carditis AND residual valvar disease — the highest-risk category requiring the longest prophylaxis.
commonExamTrap: Applying the 5-year/age 21 rule (for no carditis) or 10-year/age 21 rule (for carditis without residual disease) to a patient who has residual valvar disease.
missedConcept: Three distinct categories of secondary RF prophylaxis duration exist based on whether carditis and residual valvar disease are present.

topic: Pulmonary arterial hypertension (WHO Group I) — prognosis
clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman presents with progressive exertional dyspnoea, fatigue, and one episode of exertional syncope over 8 months. She has no history of lung or heart disease. Investigations: TTE shows an estimated right ventricular systolic pressure of 72 mmHg, dilated RA and RV. Right heart catheterisation confirms mPAP 46 mmHg, PCWP 10 mmHg, PVR 7.2 Wood units. No mutation identified. She is diagnosed with idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (IPAH). What are the approximate survival rates from the time of right heart catheterisation diagnosis?
options: 85% at 1 year, 68% at 3 years, 57% at 5 years
95% at 1 year, 90% at 3 years, 85% at 5 years
60% at 1 year, 40% at 3 years, 25% at 5 years
75% at 1 year, 50% at 3 years, 30% at 5 years
90% at 1 year, 75% at 3 years, 60% at 5 years
explanation: According to the lecture on pulmonary hypertension by Dr. Ali Jabari, the survival rates for WHO Group 1 PAH from the time of right heart catheterisation are: 85% at 1 year, 68% at 3 years, and 57% at 5 years. These figures reflect the relatively poor prognosis of PAH despite modern therapies, and underscore the importance of early diagnosis and aggressive treatment.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Survival rates for WHO Group 1 PAH: 85% at 1 year, 68% at 3 years, 57% at 5 years.
Incorrect. These figures are too optimistic; PAH has a significantly worse prognosis than these numbers suggest.
Incorrect. These figures are overly pessimistic; modern PAH therapies have improved but not normalised survival.
Incorrect. These intermediate figures do not match the published survival data presented in the lecture.
Incorrect. These figures overestimate survival; the 5-year survival of approximately 57% reflects the persistent severity of PAH despite treatment.
clinicalPearl: WHO Group 1 PAH carries a significant mortality burden: 85% 1-year, 68% 3-year, and 57% 5-year survival from time of RHC diagnosis, even with modern therapies.
keyClue: Idiopathic PAH diagnosed by RHC with elevated PVR and pre-capillary haemodynamics.
commonExamTrap: Overestimating PAH survival — it remains a serious condition with approximately 43% 5-year mortality despite available targeted therapies.
missedConcept: Survival data for WHO Group 1 PAH: 85% at 1 year, 68% at 3 years, 57% at 5 years from the time of right heart catheterisation.

topic: Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH, Group IV)
clinicalStem: A 54-year-old man had a documented massive pulmonary embolism 18 months ago, treated with anticoagulation. Despite anticoagulation, he remains severely breathless on minimal exertion (WHO functional class III). A V/Q scan shows multiple mismatched perfusion defects. Right heart catheterisation: mPAP 38 mmHg, PCWP 12 mmHg, PVR 6.1 Wood units. CT pulmonary angiography shows ring-like stenoses and organised webs in the proximal and lobar pulmonary arteries. He is otherwise fit for surgery. Which is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Pulmonary endarterectomy (PEA) — the definitive treatment for CTEPH in technically operable disease
Lifelong anticoagulation alone, as this is the first-line and only proven therapy for CTEPH
Balloon pulmonary angioplasty (BPA) — preferred over surgery as it avoids cardiopulmonary bypass
PAH-specific therapy (e.g. riociguat) as the primary treatment for all CTEPH patients
Repeat thrombolysis with IV alteplase as the obstruction is likely residual acute thrombus
explanation: Pulmonary endarterectomy (PEA) is the definitive therapy for CTEPH and should be considered in all patients with technically operable disease. The general criteria for surgery include preoperative WHO Class II–IV symptoms and surgical accessibility of thrombi (proximal/lobar disease). This patient has operable proximal disease and is fit for surgery, making PEA the treatment of choice. Lifelong anticoagulation is an essential first step but is not definitive. BPA is reserved for inoperable or residual disease after surgical treatment.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. PEA is the definitive treatment for CTEPH with technically accessible (proximal/lobar) disease and WHO Class II–IV symptoms.
Incorrect. Anticoagulation is the first step and is lifelong, but it is not definitive treatment — surgical PEA is the curative option in operable disease.
Incorrect. BPA is indicated for symptomatic, inoperable CTEPH or residual/recurrent PH after surgery — not as first-line in operable proximal disease.
Incorrect. Riociguat (and PAH-specific therapies) is used in inoperable CTEPH or as adjunct therapy; it is not the first-line definitive treatment when surgery is feasible.
Incorrect. After 18 months, the organised thromboemboli in CTEPH are fibrotic webs and stenoses, not acute thrombus; thrombolysis is ineffective and inappropriate.
clinicalPearl: Pulmonary endarterectomy is the definitive, potentially curative treatment for CTEPH with proximal/lobar accessible thrombi and WHO functional class II–IV symptoms; BPA is for inoperable or residual disease.
keyClue: Proximal lobar ring-like stenoses and webs on CTPA, pre-capillary PH on RHC, technically operable — PEA is indicated.
commonExamTrap: Choosing BPA as first-line for all CTEPH — BPA is reserved for inoperable cases or residual PH post-PEA; surgery is preferred when disease is surgically accessible.
missedConcept: In CTEPH: (1) lifelong anticoagulation — always; (2) PEA — for operable proximal disease; (3) BPA — for inoperable/distal disease; (4) riociguat — for inoperable or residual PH.
